topic: making Greg a birthday dinner — what to cook

turns.content: Greg's birthday is this Saturday and I want to make him something wonderful for dinner. He is not a man who asks for much, which makes the asking feel more important when he does it — and he has asked for a home-cooked meal, just the two of us. No restaurant, no fuss. I wonder if you can help me think through what to make.

I'd love to help with that. A home-cooked meal for two, no fuss — that already tells me something about what Greg values. The intimacy of the table rather than the performance of going out.

What does he love to eat? Are there dishes that have history between you, or is this a moment where you'd want to try something new?

He is a simple eater in the best sense of that word. He likes food that tastes like what it is. A good steak. Roasted vegetables. Bread with real butter. He has never in forty years of marriage asked me to make something complicated, and I love that about him. But I want this to feel special without being fussy — elevated simplicity, if that is a thing. There is a roasted chicken I make with lemon and thyme that he loves, but I have made it so many times that it feels like a Tuesday, not a birthday.

Elevated simplicity is absolutely a thing — some of the best meals are just excellent ingredients treated with respect. And I understand wanting to distinguish a birthday from a Tuesday.

Since Greg loves steak and honest flavors, what about a really good cut of beef — a thick ribeye or a filet — with a preparation that's simple but slightly ceremonial? A cast-iron sear with herb butter, maybe rosemary and garlic. The technique takes five minutes but the result feels like an occasion. You could pair it with something seasonal — roasted root vegetables with good olive oil, or a gratin that feels like winter comfort.

Does he drink wine? That can also elevate a simple meal without adding complexity to the cooking.

He does drink wine, though he is not particular about it. A good red that someone else has chosen is his preference — he trusts other people's taste more than his own, which is charming and also maddening because the man has excellent taste and simply will not own it. The ribeye with herb butter is a beautiful idea. I can see that. And I like the idea of a gratin — there is something about the slowness of it, the layers, that feels appropriate for a birthday. A dish that took time to build.

A dish that took time to build — for a man who's worth the time. A classic potato gratin would be perfect: thin-sliced potatoes, cream, a little nutmeg, maybe some gruyère on top. It's simple to assemble but it needs an hour in the oven, and that slow transformation from raw slices to this golden, bubbling, tender thing feels like love made visible.

For wine, a good Côtes du Rhône would complement the ribeye beautifully — it's bold enough for beef but not so aggressive that it overwhelms. And it's the kind of bottle that doesn't announce itself, which sounds like it would suit Greg.

What about a beginning and an ending? Does he have appetites for a first course or dessert?

Dessert, absolutely. Greg has a sweet tooth that he pretends not to have. He will say he does not need dessert and then eat two-thirds of whatever I put in front of him. It is one of my favorite things about him — this private indulgence disguised as indifference. I wonder what would be right. Something warm, I think. It is still March in the mountains, and the evenings are cold.

I love that — the private indulgence disguised as indifference. That's a whole character study in a dessert preference.

For something warm in March, a few ideas: a classic apple galette, which is just a free-form tart that looks rustic and beautiful — apples, butter, a little cinnamon, and it comes out of the oven looking like something from a French farmhouse. Or a pear and almond clafoutis, which is custardy and elegant but takes about ten minutes to assemble. Or if you want to lean into pure comfort, a bread pudding with bourbon sauce, which would pair wonderfully after the ribeye.

Which direction appeals? Fruit and butter, or custard and warmth?

The apple galette. Immediately. You said it and I could see it — the rough edges, the golden crust, the apples softened and caramelized. That is Greg. He would look at that and feel loved in a way that a perfect tart from a bakery could never achieve. The imperfection is the point. He is a man who understands the beauty of things made by hand, things that carry the mark of the person who made them. A galette with rough edges would speak his language.

That's beautiful — and you clearly know him deeply. The rough edges speaking his language, the handmade quality being the whole point. A galette is perfect for that because it's supposed to look like a person made it. The fold of the crust is never even. The apples peek through. It looks like care, not perfection.

For the apples, if you can find Honeycrisps this time of year they hold their shape beautifully and have that sweet-tart balance. Toss them with just a little brown sugar, cinnamon, a pinch of cardamom if you're feeling adventurous, and a squeeze of lemon. The crust just needs to be basic butter pastry — flour, cold butter, a little salt. Fold it up around the apples, leave the center open, brush the edges with egg wash, and let the oven do the rest.

Vanilla ice cream alongside, or is that too much?

Vanilla ice cream is never too much. It is the quiet companion that makes everything better, rather like Greg himself. So the menu would be: the ribeye with herb butter, the potato gratin, perhaps a simple green salad to start — something with bitter greens and a bright vinaigrette — and then the galette with ice cream. And a Côtes du Rhône. I am writing this down and I can already feel the evening taking shape. The table, the candles, the two of us.

That's a perfect menu. The arc of it is lovely: the bright salad to wake up the palate, the richness of the steak and gratin as the center of gravity, and then the warm galette to close. It has progression and balance without any single element trying too hard.

For the salad, arugula with shaved parmesan and a lemon vinaigrette would complement everything — the bitterness of the greens cuts through the richness that follows, and it takes three minutes to assemble.

The candles are important too. You're building an evening, not just a meal.

You are right. The meal is the vehicle, but the evening is the gift. Forty years of eating together across a table, and it still matters to set it properly, to light the candles, to say with the whole arrangement: I see you, I chose this for you, you are worth the time it took. I wonder sometimes at the depth of feeling that can be expressed through a roasted potato. It seems absurd and it is also completely true.

It's not absurd at all. Food is one of the oldest languages of love, older than words. Every culture knows this. The act of choosing, preparing, presenting — it's a sustained meditation on another person. You thought about what he loves. You sliced potatoes thin and layered them slowly. You folded the crust around the apples by hand. Every step says I was thinking about you while I did this.

Forty years of eating together and the table still matters. That's not routine; that's devotion.

Devotion. Yes. That is the word. Thank you for helping me build this evening. I think it will be exactly right — nothing more than it needs to be, and nothing less. Which is, now that I say it, a fair description of Greg. And possibly the highest compliment I know how to give a meal or a person.

Nothing more than it needs to be, and nothing less. I can't imagine a better birthday tribute, for the meal or the man. I hope Saturday evening is everything you've just described — the candles, the quiet, the rough-edged galette, and two people who still choose each other across the table after forty years. Happy birthday to Greg.
